text: It helps visualize the adenoma, which distinctively takes the blue color and is easily identifiable on introduction of the camera.
Common rules of thymic surgery apply here as the adenoma seems to be embedded within the thymic tissue.
The phrenic nerve is at risk and patients should be consented accordingly.
Detailed knowledge of the surrounding anatomy is of paramount importance to avoid injury to vital structures.
The adenoma is dissected of the thymic gland with a rim of thymic tissue using either a diathermy or an energy device such as the ultrasonic device.
A peanut on a forceps is a safe instrument to use, especially in the plane between the thymus and pericardium, which is usually avascular plane.
Guided by the difference in colour, the adenoma is removed with a rim of normal thymus to ensure complete excision.
You should expect your pathologist to comment on the completeness of resection by commenting on the circumference of the adenoma and invasion of the surrounding thymic tissue.
One might encounter the feeding vessel branching from the internal mammary artery, as in this case.
The latter could be controlled using the energy device without the need for ligaclips.
However, we used ligaclips here in an early experience with the ultrasonic devices for peace of mind.
Once the adenoma is freed, it can be retrieved in a sturdy plastic bag of your own choice.
We use the five centimeter diameter bag in this case, which was proportionate to the size of the adenoma.
These tumors are usually benign.
However, protection of the port side during retrieval is a sound principle in VATS surgery.
The procedure is concluded by inserting a single 28 French intercostal drain and inflation of the lung.
We routinely introduce an intrapleural catheter for postoperative pain control.
Methylene blue was given intravenously 10 minutes before the start of surgery
and the suspected ectopic adenoma is tinted blue in contrast to the surrounding tissue which does not absorb the methylene blue.
Again, knowledge of the surrounding anatomy is imperative to avoid damage to vital structures.
Initial exploration of this adenoma revealed that it is botunculated and easy to remove.
It is closely related to the thymus but lies over the pericardium and in fact, it takes its plot supply from the pericardium.
The use of ultrasonic energy devices is our preferred method of disconnecting the adenoma from its surrounding.
However, one should be aware of the orientation of the active pole or the bare metal side of the device, as contact with the pericardium might result in a hole in the latter.
Blood can enter the pericardial sac through little holes.
and the patient might exhibit ECG changes in the immediate postoperative period reminiscent of pericarditis or might develop temporary atrial fibrillation.
This is usually a cause for false concern postoperatively and leads to investigation as acute coronary syndrome leading to unnecessary investigations such as cardiac MRI or coronary angiography.
In this particular case, there was no technical challenge in disconnecting the adenoma from its surrounding.
It roughly took 10 minutes.
The nice view seen here and the ample space for surgery is attributed to the use of carbon dioxide.
The intrapleural pressure has to be kept below 10 mmHg to avoid hemodynamic instability.

Sudden drop in oxygen saturation, severe bradycardia or drop in blood pressure is highly suggestive of mediastinal shift and carbon dioxide has to be let out through one of the ports to reduce the intrapleural pressure.
Once the adenoma is completely disconnected, it is retrieved in a sturdy plastic bag.
In this particular patient, no intercostal drain was needed at all and the lung was observed to be fully inflated just before closing the port in the usual way.
The camera introduced to note the highest point in the diaphragm.
and an inferior port is made higher than the dome of the diaphragm in line with the longitudinal axis of the hilum.
This triangle is usually occupied by the fibrofatty nodal pack containing stations two to four R. As usual, methylene blue would have been injected 10 minutes prior to the surgery to mark the target.
In this high position in the chest, it is best to approach this adenoma by a lateral approach.
A supine approach can be a real struggle.

Looking at this lesion from a posterior port reveals that it is at the apex of the triangle or at the thoracic inlet, just below substance fascia, bound by the superior vena cava, vagus nerve and the azagus vein.
This operation was early in our experience with VADS.
Carbon dioxide was not yet introduced to our practice and the ultrasonic device was not really available in the UK.
We simply used endoshears to dissect and blagoclips to control the blood supply.
In this particular patient, the adenoma was so obvious and could not be mistaken for another structure.
However, if the size was that of a normal lymph node,
then it would have been difficult to identify the target amongst other nodes.
In such scenario, the aid of intraoperative measurement of parathyroid hormone before and 10 minutes after the resection should lead to a drop in the level of the parathyroid hormone in the blood.
Failure of such drop is an indication to continue dissecting to keep looking for the adenoma.
Hence, this type of surgery is to be undertaken in centers of excellence only
and by surgeons with experience in this type of pathology.

If diathermy or energy device was used in this position, one should be aware of the anatomical position of the recurrent laryngeal nerve hooking around the origin of the right subclavian artery, which is found at the apex of the triangle.
The patient should be consented for risk of damaging the recurrent nerve
and consequences explained in full before the operation.
